header.title: Stack
header.description: A stack is a linear data structure that enforces LIFO — Last In, First Out. Every core operation (push, pop, peek) runs in O(1), making stacks the go-to tool for parenthesis matching, function call tracking, undo history, and monotonic-stack patterns.

howItWorks.title: How a stack works
items: Elements are added and removed from one end only — the top. This constraint is what gives a stack its power and predictability.
Push places a new element on top in O(1) — no shifting, just a pointer increment.
Pop removes and returns the top element in O(1) — again, a single pointer move.
Peek reads the top without removing it — useful when you need to inspect before deciding whether to pop.
Searching for an arbitrary value requires scanning from the top downward — O(n) — because stacks offer no random access.
The call stack in every programming language is literally a stack: each function call pushes a frame; returning pops it.

files.code:
# ─── Stack in Python ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
#
# Python's list doubles as a stack — append/pop at the end are O(1) amortized.
# collections.deque is faster for very large stacks (no occasional O(n) resize).

from collections import deque

# ── Using list (most common) ──────────────────────────────────────────────
stack = []
stack.append(10)  # push  → [10]
stack.append(20)  # push  → [10, 20]
stack.append(30)  # push  → [10, 20, 30]

top     = stack[-1]   # peek  → 30  (no removal)
removed = stack.pop() # pop   → 30, stack = [10, 20]
empty   = len(stack) == 0  # False

# ── Using deque (preferred for performance-critical code) ─────────────────
dq = deque()
dq.append(10)       # push
dq.append(20)
top     = dq[-1]    # peek  → 20
removed = dq.pop()  # pop   → 20

# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Pattern 1 — Balanced parentheses                          O(n) time/space
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
def is_balanced(s: str) -> bool:
    stack = []
    match = {')': '(', ']': '[', '}': '{'}
    for ch in s:
        if ch in '([{':
            stack.append(ch)
        elif ch in ')]}':
            if not stack or stack.pop() != match[ch]:
                return False
    return not stack

# is_balanced("({[]})") → True
# is_balanced("({[})") → False

# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Pattern 2 — Monotonic stack (next greater element)        O(n) time/space
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
def next_greater(nums: list[int]) -> list[int]:
    result = [-1] * len(nums)
    stack = []  # stores indices
    for i, val in enumerate(nums):
        while stack and nums[stack[-1]] < val:
            result[stack.pop()] = val
        stack.append(i)
    return result

# next_greater([2, 1, 2, 4, 3]) → [4, 2, 4, -1, -1]

# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
# Pattern 3 — Min stack (getMin in O(1))                    O(n) space
# ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
class MinStack:
    """Track the current minimum alongside each pushed value."""
    def __init__(self):
        self._stack = []        # (value, current_min) pairs

    def push(self, val: int) -> None:
        min_so_far = min(val, self._stack[-1][1]) if self._stack else val
        self._stack.append((val, min_so_far))

    def pop(self) -> int:
        return self._stack.pop()[0]

    def peek(self) -> int:
        return self._stack[-1][0]

    def get_min(self) -> int:
        return self._stack[-1][1]   # O(1) — stored at each level

footerAlerts.title: When stacks shine
items: Balanced parentheses / bracket matching — push opens, pop on close and verify.
Monotonic stack — maintain a decreasing/increasing sequence to find next greater/smaller in O(n).
Expression evaluation (RPN, infix parsing) — operands on stack, apply operator when seen.
Undo / redo — push actions onto two stacks; swap on undo/redo.
DFS iteratively — replace the call stack with an explicit stack to avoid recursion limits.
Min/max stack — track running min or max in O(1) per operation by storing it alongside each value.

footerAlerts.title: When to consider alternatives
items: Need FIFO ordering → use a Queue or Deque instead.
Need random access by index → use an Array.
Need O(log n) min/max with arbitrary inserts → use a Heap (priority queue).
